question: What is the primary problem your project aims to solve?
answer: All of the above: (1) Bridge planning-to-execution gap — Sherpy currently creates implementation plans but has no mechanism to track, manage, or schedule work across multiple projects after planning is complete. (2) Multi-project visibility — Need a unified view of all active projects across repositories to understand workload, conflicts, and priorities. (3) Resource-constrained scheduling — Teams and individuals work on multiple projects simultaneously and need AI-assisted scheduling to optimize allocation.

question: What is the core value proposition of Sherpy PM?
answer: All of the above: (1) Single source of truth — Transform Sherpy from a planning-only tool into a full lifecycle project management system where plans become living, tracked projects with real-time status. (2) AI-optimized delivery — Leverage AI not just for planning but for ongoing scheduling optimization, resource rebalancing, and risk prediction across a portfolio of projects. (3) Developer-first PM — A project management tool that lives where developers work (CLI + local files), not a separate web app that creates context-switching overhead.

question: What is included in the initial scope?
answer: Core + resource management + AI scheduling — Full feature set: Global Node.js CLI, local SQLite database, project registration from .sherpy folders, basic CRUD for projects/tasks, status tracking, relative path references to planning docs, team member profiles, user assignments, capacity tracking, workload dashboards, AI-assisted scheduling, conflict detection, resource rebalancing, and risk prediction.

question: What should be explicitly excluded from the initial release?
answer: All of the above: (1) Web UI, cloud sync, multi-user collaboration — Keep it local-first and single-user focused; no web dashboard, no cloud database, no real-time sync between users. (2) Advanced reporting, Gantt charts, time tracking — Exclude visualization-heavy features; focus on data model and AI scheduling as the differentiator. (3) Integration with external tools (Jira, GitHub, Slack) — No integrations in v1; Sherpy PM is self-contained.

question: Who are the primary users of Sherpy PM?
answer: Solo developer/tech lead managing multiple projects — An individual who plans and executes work across several repositories and needs visibility into overall workload and scheduling.

question: What are the primary goals of this user?
answer: Track planning-to-completion lifecycle — Know where each project stands relative to its original plan (on track, at risk, delayed). Additionally: ensure correct planning of all developers they manage and give higher-ups visibility into the reasoning for current schedules.

question: What are the biggest pain points this user faces today?
answer: All of the above: (1) Plans rot after creation — Sherpy generates great plans but they live in static files. Once planning ends, there is no mechanism to track progress against the plan or surface drift. (2) No cross-project visibility — Each project is siloed in its own repo/folder. No way to see what are all my active projects and how they are doing without manually checking each one. (3) Scheduling is manual and error-prone — Allocating developers across projects, balancing workloads, and adjusting when things slip is done in spreadsheets or heads, not systematically.

question: What is the most important use case for Sherpy PM?
answer: (1) Register and track projects — Point the CLI at a repo with .sherpy folder, it ingests the planning docs, registers the project in SQLite, and begins tracking status against milestones. (2) AI-assisted schedule generation — Input available developers, their skills, and time constraints; AI produces an optimal schedule across all registered projects. Additionally: the ability to run planning scenarios for new projects with different developers to understand how to best organize/schedule upcoming projects.

question: What other key use cases should be supported?
answer: (1) Milestone/task status updates — Mark milestones and tasks as in-progress, blocked, complete; update actual vs estimated effort. (2) Resource conflict detection — System warns when a developer is over-allocated or when two projects compete for the same resource window. (3) What-if rescheduling — When a project slips or a developer becomes unavailable, recalculate the optimal schedule and show impact.

question: What is the core data model the CLI should manage?
answer: Projects, milestones, tasks, people, assignments — A relational model where projects contain milestones and tasks, people have skills/capacity, and assignments link people to tasks with time allocation.

question: How should projects reference their planning documents?
answer: Use the Sherpy CLI to run the sherpy flow and create all documents inside the .sherpy folder (not scattered around the codebase). Documents organized by folders with {project-id}-{slug} naming convention. This keeps all planning artifacts consolidated and discoverable by the CLI.

question: What AI-assisted scheduling capabilities are essential?
answer: Optimal assignment given constraints — Input: available people, their skills, availability windows, project priorities. Output: recommended task assignments and schedule that maximizes throughput while respecting constraints.

question: How should the CLI surface schedule and status information to users?
answer: Not a pure CLI — instead a locally running API daemon with a web interface (React) using assistant-ui. The CLI manages the daemon lifecycle (start/stop) but the primary interaction surface is a local web app that provides rich visualization and AI chat-based interaction for scheduling and status.

question: How should the local API daemon and web interface work together?
answer: CLI starts daemon + opens browser — sherpy start launches the API server, serves the React app, and opens the browser. sherpy stop gracefully shuts down. SQLite accessed only through the API layer.

question: What performance expectations do you have for the local system?
answer: All of the above: (1) Instant for local scale — All queries return in <100ms since it is local SQLite with a handful of projects. AI scheduling calls may take 5-15 seconds and should show progress. (2) Real-time dashboard updates — Web UI should auto-refresh when data changes. WebSocket-based push. (3) Offline-first with sync awareness — Must work fully offline. If multi-machine sync is added later, the architecture should support it but it is not in v1.

question: What security considerations apply to a locally-running system?
answer: Localhost-only binding — API binds to 127.0.0.1 only. No authentication needed for v1 since it is single-user local. SQLite file has standard filesystem permissions.

question: How will you measure success for Sherpy PM?
answer: Success is measured by ability to: (1) Create a new project plan with sherpy tools using AI. (2) See all active initiatives and their status and timelines. (3) See by developer or project the resource allocations and be able to estimate when projects will complete. (4) Deal with delays and re-prioritizations easily via the UI. (5) See how to schedule new project work given current resource limitations.

question: What technical constraints apply to this project?
answer: Node.js + SQLite only — Must be a Node.js CLI/app with SQLite. No external databases, no cloud services (beyond AI API calls). React for the web UI. Additionally: backend fully built using Effect-TS. Frontend is latest React using Tailwind, shadcn, and assistant-ui. Backend integrates only with AWS Bedrock using the AI SDK from Vercel.

question: What timeline constraints apply?
answer: ASAP — No fixed timeline but the highest priority is getting something usable as quickly as possible. Ship fast, iterate.

question: What external dependencies does this project rely on?
answer: (1) AWS Bedrock for AI — All AI scheduling and scenario analysis goes through AWS Bedrock via Vercel AI SDK. No other AI provider. (3) Node.js ecosystem — Use Effect-TS with @effect/sqlite-libsql (not better-sqlite3). Reference patterns from ~/Sites/ai-use-repos/effect and ~/Sites/ai-use-repos/EffectPatterns.

question: What internal dependencies exist (other tools, teams, processes)?
answer: No changes needed to Sherpy skills — the API calling Claude via Bedrock should declare the location for the files to be saved. The planning skills remain unchanged; Sherpy PM controls where outputs land.

question: What assumptions are you making about this project?
answer: All of the above: (1) Users already use Sherpy for planning — The tool assumes projects have been or will be planned using the Sherpy flow. It does not help manage unplanned/ad-hoc work. (2) AWS Bedrock stays available and affordable — AI scheduling depends on Bedrock being accessible and cost-effective for individual use. (3) Local-first is acceptable for v1 — Users are okay with data living on their machine. No cloud backup or multi-device sync.

question: What risks concern you most for this project?
answer: AI scheduling accuracy — AI-generated schedules may be poor initially, requiring iteration on prompts and constraints modeling.

question: What delivery phases do you envision?
answer: Vertical slice first — Build one complete end-to-end workflow (e.g., register project, see it in UI, assign people) then expand.
